context.scenario:
I’m in a computer systems servicing class, and we have a rule where we’re not supposed to be playing games on the computers during class time. I was assigned a computer with administrator privileges, so I can monitor the other computers in the lab. One day, I noticed that my classmate was playing a game on their computer. Since we’re not supposed to be doing that, I decided to check on it using my admin privileges to confirm if they were actually playing a game or if I was just imagining it.

When I checked, I saw that they were indeed playing a game, so I decided to mess with their computer to let them know they were breaking the rule. I didn’t delete anything or do anything too extreme, but I did interfere with their work.

Afterward, my classmate reacted to what I did, but they didn’t say anything specific. They didn’t seem upset or anything, so I figured it wasn’t a big deal. I thought maybe they were just brushing it off.

Later, I told the teacher that my classmate was playing a game on their computer. The teacher responded by saying that they were actually looking at their presentation. That’s when I realized that what I thought was a game was actually part of their presentation. At that point, I felt really guilty because I had messed with something that was important to them, and I didn’t even realize it at the time.

I know I was trying to follow the class rules, but I feel like I might have overstepped. I didn’t mean to mess up their presentation, but I still feel bad about it. AITA for messing with their computer, even though I thought they were playing games? Even though they did not say anything negative I still feel like the Asshole.
context.comment:
YTA - *I know I was trying to follow the class rules,*

At no time were you following the class rules because I'm willing to bet that the rules also say that you are to report or discipline the person.  Not mess with them.  That is immature and an abuse of your power.

  
*but I feel like I might have overstepped.*

You 100% did overstep because YOU didn't follow the rules on when something like this happens.

You got caught in a power trip.  You will see that later in life, you will get a position that does give you some power over people.  The important thing to know about that is that with power comes responsibility.  You've shown that you can not be trusted in the position that you're in.

context.scenario: I (M 33) am watching my boyfriend’s dog while he’s away for work for about a month. My roommate (M 34) is having a stressful week at work and works most days from our apartment. On Monday and Tuesday, I went to the gym to work out with my friend who’s a personal trainer and training me for free on his time. So, I left at 4:30 and came back at 6:30 and my roommate said he cannot handle dog watching while I’m away and he’s working. This is the calmest and least needy dog I’ve ever met and he’s a 10 years old yorkipoo. He just sleeps and occasionally barks when he hears our neighbors coming and going. My roommate said that he can’t mentally separate having a dog hanging out at home or if I had brought home a guest and then left him alone to entertain my friend. I just needed 2 hours away to workout. My roommate was at the office most of the day Monday and Tuesday and then came home and had more work to do. He came home just as I was leaving for the gym at 4:30 and I came home and was lectured more about how I didn’t listen to him and left him alone to dog sit again. I don’t see how this is dog sitting; he doesn’t have to walk, feed, or even interact with the dog. So, am I the asshole for not understanding where he’s coming from and still trying to go to the gym for my 2 hours?
context.comment:
YTA - You're being very entitled with your roommate, OP.  YOU signed up to watch the dog, not your roommate.  When you asked if it was ok for YOU to bring the dog over so YOU can watch it, he said that's fine.  What he didn't say was that it was fine to just up and leave the dog at home and inconvenience him while you go out and are not inconvenienced by the dog.  You are making soooo many generalizations here.  

*I don’t see how this is dog sitting; he doesn’t have to walk, feed, or even interact with the dog.*

You're right.  He doesn't have to.  But if anything happens while you're not at home taking care of the dog YOU agreed to take care of, you think you're roommate is just going to sit there and let whatever is happening to the dog happen?  Really?  It's about being present because the dog is there at the home and you're not.

Also, if he's working this is distracting as hell:

*occasionally barks when he hears our neighbors coming and going.*

You taking it so lightly is very disrespectful to your roommate and makes you even more of an AH.

Just because you don't have a problem with it does NOT mean that everyone else doesn't have a problem with it.  And you don't get to tell them that it's not that big of a deal.  They will tell you.  This is gaslighting.  Read the room, OP.  Your roommate doesn't want to be left alone with the dog at all.  And that's perfectly fine.  If you're all bent out of shape over it then YOU need to find a solution without judgement for the people who tell you "no" when asked.  So show some respect and stop leaving the dog at home.  Also, don't get passive aggressive with the respect thing and throw it back at him at a later date.

context.scenario:
I’m (17F) supposed to be doing a play in a few weeks. I’m the lead role and I’m very happy about that. My health has been very bad lately. I have missed 2 rehearsals because of it. But I’ve been to the other like 10. Until a few days ago I got rushed to hospital because I was having extreme heart and stomach pain and vomiting. I emailed the director and told him I was in hospital so wouldn’t be able to make it to the rehearsal which was the next morning but a few hours before the rehearsal I was discharged and given medication to take for a week which they think will most likely make the symptoms better by next week. I ended up deciding to try to go to the rehearsal but halfway through the pain started again and I was feeling extremely dizzy and was slurring my words I was that unwell.

I apologised and said I need to go home. The director said ok but while I was waiting for the taxi asked if I’m genuinely sick or trying to get out of rehearsals because I don’t like the play and how it’s important for me to be there and I need to think about how my absence affects everyone else. I do like the play and obviously I want to do it and don’t want to let people down. And I said this and how I feel genuinely awful and my family told me I shouldn’t come at all but I wanted to anyway because I really didn’t want to leave them down. And he said ok health is important but it doesn’t look very professional. And I again apologised saying I’ll make sure to be better for the next rehearsal which is tommorow. 

I feel really bad for leaving them the other day and them having to do the rest of the rehearsal without me. The assistant director did email me though asking if I’m doing ok now and he could tell I wasn’t myself at all during the rehearsal and to rest well and he’ll get me up to date on what they did at the next rehearsal and until then to make sure to rest up and do whatever the doctors said and stuff. So I guess not everyone thinks Im an asshole. But yeah I still feel really awful because I don’t want to let people down.
context.comment: NTA - at some point you are going to have to make the decision to either suck it up or bow out.  Right now it's looking like you want to suck it up but your health is telling you otherwise.  This is why productions have an understudy in case anything happens to the lead.  I know you want to do this play.  I know you don't want to let the others down.  If you are not as prepared as the rest of the cast, you will need to step down in order for the play to go on.  It's not about you, it's about the production.  It's not fair to bring down the value of the show to the audience and the cast members just because "you want".  I wish you all the best and hope that those meds really do help so that none of what I said here becomes relevant.  Break a leg, OP!

context.scenario:
I (f29) am currently living at my parents house due to previous financial difficulties and the housing market being crap. I pay rent and help with bills and shopping etc. When I first moved in a few years ago 2 of my siblings (both m, and teenagers at the time)were still living here and they had the two double rooms. I moved into the spare room/box room with no complaints, just happy to have a roof over my head. This has been the way we've lived for about 5 years now. 

Now comes the tricky part. The elder of my 2 brothers, 19m, 'moved out' to go stay with an elderly relative and they are struggling atm. This started about 6/7 months ago. He took almost everything with him barring his furniture and TV. Despite working full time, he has never nor will ever contribute towards household bills (not that he is expected to while he isn't living here) so I broached the topic with my parents recently of me swapping rooms considering that my brothers larger room is empty. They were happy to let me, no problems, but we did let my brother know the plan, out of common courtesy. He blew his lid. Complaining we had no right to do that and that we could go in his room and touch his stuff (we didn't, as the door has remained locked since he left) and said that I didn't need a bigger room as all I had was junk. (I do have a decent amount of stuff, but I'm a girl with hobbies who had to quickly downsize from a house to a box room with little notice). He also said that if I was that annoyed about having a small room I should just move in with my partner (we are not at that point in our relationship and I'm not going push the situation unless things here get dire). 

This all started a few days ago and as of now I'm still in the box room but my brother has reluctantly given in an says he will move his own stuff, and not let us touch it. We, my parents and I, aren't kicking him out, he is welcome to move back in if he wanted to, however I was just making the point that I am well within my rights to move into that room considering I live here full time and pay bills and he does not. Also our relatives place is about 10mins drive away so it's not like it's putting him out having to come here and move his stuff although we offered to do it for him. 

Part of me feels bad, like he did have that room before me, but the rest of me feels justified. AITA?
context.comment: YTA - the fact that you are inconvenienced should be the tell tale sign that YOU need to get your shit together and move out of your parents house.  You even said that your brother's situation may not be permanent and that he could very well move back.  Are you going to give him his room back if that's the case?  You're 29.  Stop focusing on your room and start focusing on your life.  Stop trying to get comfortable in a place that you are only supposed to be at temporarily.  No excuses.  Focus on the big picture and quit being a burden on your family with YOUR problems.  Time to start adulting.

context.scenario:
WIBTA?? For context, I (19) live in a pet friendly townhome. I have 3 roommates, but only two really matter in this (both 20/21). I specifically chose to live here (first place i've lived since I moved out) because it's cat friendly. I almost got a cat before I left home but decided to wait until I moved in because that would be a lot of stress for a cat in my first month or two of owning it. I have always known I was going to get a cat because I've had them all my life and I love them. Onto the problem. Roommate A has a dog, so I text in our roommate groupchat and ask how her dog is with cats, so I could see if when I did get the cat I could let it out of my room (my room is plenty big enough for a cat lol). Roommate A's dog doesn't do well with cats. Ok, I say I'll keep it in my room, so then I ask if anyone's allergic to double check, not thinking anyone living in a PET FRIENDLY apartment will be ALLERGIC TO PETS. Roommate A says "I'm allergic to some breeds" and roommate B says "I'm slightly allergic to cats." From what those imply, neither is allergic to the point that it would be so dangerous I couldn't even keep the cat in my room. And for anyone worried, the room is big enough and I'm home basically all day every day so the cat isn't going to be lonely. I think I am going to move forward with getting the cat, even though two roommates are allergic. WIBTA?

Stop being rude in the comments please. For clarification: These are townhomes. Everyone in each individual townhome had to either agree or disagree on pets. All of my roommates agreed. For everyone talking about space, the cat I am looking to adopt likes going on walks. I will walk the goddamn cat I'm not evil. And My room, which is big enough for a cat to be fine on space (do you want me to buy it a mansion?) has its own bathroom and walk in closet.

more info: I had zero contact with my roommates before we moved in. I didn't know about the dog until I was actually moving in. No way I could have told them about my potential future cat before we signed the lease.

more info, because you all seem to assume I'm some sort of incompetent imbecile- when I say I am almost always home, I leave home 3 hours a day for class, and maybe 3 days a week 3 more hours for work. That 6 hours max id be away from the cat, less than half the week.

ALLERGIES ARE MILD AT WORST.

Final update: None of my roommates had said no before this post. One of them just said no. Guess what I’m not doing? being a  jerkwad dickhole like all of you are. A roommate said no so I’m not getting it. Men and your audacity is disgusting. I can tell half of you didn’t even read it (probably because you’re illiterate, and if you managed to overcome that, you’re too dense to comprehend much more than simple statements.) And those who did read it also struggle with basic human empathy and consideration of situations other than your own. Get some brains and take a fucking shower.
context.comment: YTA - all agree or no cat.  Point blank.  You cannot bring an animal into a living situation unless all are on board.  Being in a pet friendly place does not mean that you have a free pass at getting a pet.  No no no no.  Does not even remotely work that way.  If they have allergies, you don't get to decide how severe they are for your own purposes.  You asked and got your answer.  No cats.  You need to accept this and come to terms with it or start looking for a new place.

context.scenario:
In my friend group, we have one friend who always drives. Two of us don’t have cars, I get driving anxiety, and the girl who drives is really comfortable with it. 

If we’re going on longer trips, we split gas evenly, but I never paid for gas otherwise. I figured that she would be going anyway and it doesn’t add expense to take the rest of us along. We all live under 10 min apart too so drop offs aren’t hard. But I recently realized that the two other girls have been sending a few bucks or buying her coffee every so often for driving all the time. And they were shocked that I wasn’t especially because we do go to the other side of town pretty often. Basically said it was an AH move of me. One of them commented that I probably owe a pretty large amount over the few years we’ve known each other.

That friend told the driving friend, who brushed it off and told her it’s fine, but that friend is now saying it’s because she is non-confrontational and I need to be paying without being asked. Now our dynamics in the group are very awkward and stressful.

I’m kind of taken aback with all of this because our friend has never once asked for gas money. Doesn’t even ask for parking but we automatically pay her for it. I know that she also makes more money than the rest of us and is very frugal so financially she’s in a good spot and does not need it. 

I didn’t really think it was necessary because she would be spending the gas money anyway even if we drove separately. And she’s made it clear she doesn’t need it. AITA for not offering to pay for gas?
context.comment:
YTA - Hard to believe that you just thought that you get to ride for free without any compensation.  An extra body in the car uses more gas than if it was just the driver because of the weight.  Also, it might be just a little off the path but over time that adds up.  Come on, OP you can't be this naive.  Or is entitlement.  She's doing you and your friends a solid and you can't even put in the minimalist effort the return the favor.  But what really makes you TAH was this:

*I know that she also makes more money than the rest of us and is very frugal so financially she’s in a good spot and does not need it.*

You don't get to speak on behalf of other people's money ever.  It is absolutely none of your business.  She could have millions in the bank account but it doesn't mean that YOU get to decide where it's spent.  Do better, OP.

context.scenario:
My fiancée and I have been partners for 2 years and have our wedding in June. We live a 40 minute drive from each other. So we meet regularly but on days we don't meet we make up for it with a long call before going to bed.

Last night, I was reading up on a design document for a meeting that occurred at 9 am today. It wasn't something that needed me 100% mentally since it was going to be presented to us today anyway, but I needed to at least go over it. My fiancée called and I started talking to her on speaker phone. We were just discussing our day, and then we were talking about the floral setup. At some point she asked why I was giving one word answers. I apologized, told her I was reading the design document and asked her to keep talking, I'm listening. She said ok and we continued our conversation about the floral setup and I was agreeing with her. We ended our call on a happy note. 

Today she had been giving me one word responses to our morning texts. I noticed it but just thought it was a hard day at work maybe. When I called her during lunch, she was doing the same. Before we ended the call I asked her about it if alls good at work, and she said it wasn't work she was angry with me for how I behaved last night. That she felt like a burden when I asked her to keep talking. I told her that's ridiculous she knows how much I love talking to her, I just wanted to have our conversation and do my work at the same time. I had to go back in for a meeting so I unfortunately had to end the call unresolved. I haven't spoken to her yet, but I will be when I get back home. AITA?
context.comment: YTA - she was going over things for YOUR wedding day.  You know how important that is.  The only thing you did was show her that you could care less for the time and effort she's putting into one of if not the most important day of your lives.  Think about it, she goes out, spends time finding a floral shop/arrangement she likes and wants to hear what you think about it.  And then when it comes time to present them to you, she's hit with "yeah, I'm doing something else but carry on, I can hear you."  Dude, read the room.

context.scenario:
My girlfriend (24F) and I (26M) have been together for a little over a year. We get along really well most of the time, but she does this one thing that drives me absolutely mental, she constantly mispronounces common words. 

It’s not like she’s joking or trying to be funny, she genuinely thinks she’s saying them correctly. I’ve tried correcting her so many times, but it’s like it goes in one ear and out the other. Within minutes, she’ll go back to saying the wrong version again. It’s not even hard words. She’ll say stuff like “Chick-fil-ee” instead of “Chick-fil-a,” “liberry” instead of “library,” or “cipatol” when she means “capitol.” 

It is worse when it’s personal things—like the city I’m from or places I love. We’re in “Chiago” instead of Chicago, where I grew up, to visit my family right now, and it’s like she doesn’t even care. I don’t think it’s a learning thing, either. She doesn’t have dyslexia or any speach issues; she’s just lazy about it, at least that's how it feels to me. She very smart, studying for her master's while working right now, so I don’t get why this is so hard. 

This all came to a head today while we’re staying with my parents for new years. She pronounced Chicago wrong again in front of my family, then later did the same thing with my favorite restaurant. I lost my cool and snapped at her. I told her it’s frustrating that she can’t even try to get it right, especially when it’s stuff that matters to me. I know I probably sounded harsh, but I was just so angry. I feel like a jerk, because when we talk, it's like I'm scolding a little kid and not a 24-year-old woman who should know how to pronounce simple stuff by now. 

She got really quiet after that and has barely spoken to me since we got in. She's been on her phone the whole time and now I’m starting to feel like maybe I went too far. Am I the asshole for constantly correcting her or for snapping at her today?

Edit: More info. I'm going to put this here because it keeps coming up. We both grew up in northern parts of the US, so we virtually have the same typical "American" accent. We both only speak English. We are both white. She does not have dyslexia, vision loss, or hearing loss, but there could be things I'm not aware of. 

She will mispronounce most types of words: cities, restaurants, places we go, things we do, etc. She is able to pronounce these words correctly sometimes at first, but might later change her mind because "they sound better the way she says them" or "they are easier to say her way". There are a few words she misspoke when we first met, which I thought was cute at first. But it happens so often now, over the year we've been together, that sometimes I don't understand what she says, and she can't or won't explain what she really means. She speaks this way around me, her friends, and our families. 

I know I'm in the wrong for yelling at her, and I will apologise for that when she wakes up, but I just want to know why she keeps doing this. If it's condition, fine, I'm a jerk, but if she's doing this for some other reason I want to know why.
context.comment:
YTA - *She doesn’t have dyslexia or any speach issues; she’s just lazy about it, at least that's how it feels to me*

How would you know a learning disorder from a hole in your head if you're not trained and licensed?  This just sounds like you're too lazy to put in any effort to find out what's going on and that you just want her to do what you want.  Honestly, what she is doing sounds exactly like dyslexia.  Look into it.  Remember what Walt Whitman said:

Be curious, not judgmental.

context.scenario:
When my (28f) sister (25f) graduated high school, she worked for a year then traveled for a year.  She just did a retail job and DoorDash and she claims she made about $40k and saved it since she was living at home. She then “car camped” across the continental US, backpacked across Southeast Asia, some of Africa and the Middle East, most of Europe, and parts of South/Central America. She said her trip total was around $20k.

She came back and attended community college for a year and half and got her associate’s. 

My sister told me that our parents (50s) gifted her $1k for an emergency fund before traveling. My parents gifted me $1k for an emergency fund as well during my first year of college. She also said that our parents bought her textbooks for college which my parents also did for me. 

It just isn’t adding up to me. It feels like my parents have always favored her and I think they’re gave her more money to travel than they’re letting on. I tried confronting my parents, but they’re swear up and down that they only gave my sister $1k. She didn’t seem to work that much during her year off and spent more time going to parties at a college she didn’t even attend, on dates, planning her trip, and volunteering at the humane society. I think she spent more time doing unpaid labor at the shelter than doing actual paid work.

Now my sister’s boyfriend (28m) is moving to Switzerland for work for a year and she decided she’s going with him. My parents gifted them $1k to help with moving expenses. When I heard about this from my youngest sister (16f) I lost it. They’ve never given me a dime since I graduated college. When I confronted my parents, my mom told me it was because my sister reached a milestone in her life and if I decided to move across an ocean or have a kid or buy a house, they would give me a gift too.

I’ve reached several milestones in my life since graduating college (buying a car, running a half marathon, getting a pet) and I’ve never seen any money.

Now my parents invited me over for a goodbye party for my sister and her boyfriend. I declined the invitation and told my parents to tell my sister that I no longer wish to see her. My parents told me I’m being unreasonable and even said they’d give me money if I “needed it that bad.” It’s not that I need the money, it’s just the principle.

My sister has reached out several times, but I’ve ignored her. 

AITA for this?
context.comment:
YTA - *(buying a car, running a half marathon, getting a pet)*

lol....milestones. 

None of those are milestones.  They are accomplishments, not milestones.  Graduating college is a milestone(you got money for that), buying a new house is a milestone.  Getting married is a milestone.  Having a child is a milestone.  How is getting a pet a milestone?!?!.  You basically need a pulse and a roof to have a pet.  Far from a "milestone"  You just sound completely jealous of your sister because she is living her life the way SHE wants to.  Also, it's none of your business how much your parents give her.  It's their money.  They can spend how they see fit.  Especially when we're talking about such a small amount.  It's not a life changing amount.  Get over yourself and get out of your head.  You just went no contact with your sister over this.  Hope a couple of bucks was worth it.

context.scenario:
My sister asked me to book dinner reservations for her partner’s birthday, which she was supposed to organize. I secured a 5 PM reservation for 7 people at a popular restaurant and informed everyone three days in advance. The night before, she asked if I could move it to 6 PM because her partner had taken a last-minute work shift and wouldn’t make it at 5. We were both frustrated, and she likely argued with him over it. Since the restaurant was always booked, I told her I’d try but warned it was unlikely. When I asked about a backup plan, she said that I should just cancel it. She added that her partner was annoying for not being able to say no to work and that she was tired of doing things for people who couldn’t be bothered to help out.

The next morning, I called the restaurant, and they couldn’t move the reservation, so I canceled it, assuming the dinner was off. But then my sister decided we should still celebrate at 6:30 PM at another restaurant. At this point, I was frustrated by all the last-minute changes and questioned why we were going out of our way for her partner when he didn’t seem to care. She defended him, saying he had no family here and would have to spend his birthday alone, and that we shouldn’t blame him for taking an extra shift to make more money.

I suggested a restaurant, but she rejected it, choosing one she’d previously dismissed for “bad ambiance.” When I pointed this out, she got defensive, citing her exhaustion and travel. I asked why she planned dinner on the same day she was traveling, and my other sister and I suggested Sunday lunch, but she refused, saying her partner wasn’t available. When asked if dinner was canceled, she exploded, blaming us for not helping and claiming she had no Wi-Fi on her flight.

Later, she said her partner was available again, making me question why I’d canceled the original reservation. She yelled that no one could get ready for a 5 PM dinner and accused us of not caring, even though I’d followed her instructions. My other sister, who had rearranged her schedule, was also frustrated.

My sister then lashed out, saying she’d tell her partner no one cared about him. I reminded her she told me to cancel and had proof. She ranted about being the only one making an effort, despite me working two jobs and our other sister having a child. I decided not to go, and my other sister backed out too. My sister lost it, saying she was sick of convincing people and ended with a rage-filled message, telling us to go f\*\*\* ourselves and saying she hopes we live a life of trouble and misery.

I tried to accommodate her, but she kept changing plans and blaming everyone. AITA for refusing to keep adjusting and backing out?
context.comment:
NTA - *My sister asked me to book dinner reservations for her partner’s birthday,*

So instead of calling the restaurant that she wanted the dinner at, she called YOU and asked you to call the restaurant.  You got set up, OP.  She knew what she was doing from the start.  See, if she had done it, it would have gone off without a hitch.  But since it was you doing it, it was never going to be enough and fuel for her manipulative rage.

context.scenario:
I have two friends who were taking care of my kids while I work. They were paid through a program (not directly by me), and this arrangement had been going on for a while without issues.

For the last couple of years, I’ve been saving money to buy a house. A few months ago, my daughter, who is a full-time college student and doesn’t work, told me she had accumulated about $4,000 in debt. She asked me to help pay it off. I was hesitant because I knew it would delay my goal of buying a home, but I agreed to help her—partly because her older sister convinced me to.

We had an agreement that she would start paying me back within 3 months. That time passed, and she hadn’t made any payments. So, I decided to let my two friends go and give the job to my daughter so she could start earning money and repay the debt.

My friends are upset and have stopped talking to me. They said I took away their income just to fix a problem my daughter created, and that she should’ve figured out how to handle her debt without involving them. I explained that I need the money back, and this was the only real solution I could come up with.

AITA?
context.comment: YTA - and your friends are 100% correct.  You took away their income to ENABLE your daughter.  What lesson is she supposed to learn when mommy comes and bails her out of her responsibilities?  She's an adult and needs to learn how to be an adult.  Just because you have a timeline for buying a house means nothing.  Your daughter got herself into debt, you paid it off and didn't put it in writing(she's an adult, yes you can put it in writing) and have now put hardship on two(2) families that you considered a friend.  You would never hear from me ever again.

scenario:
I (24M) carpool to work with a coworker, Megan (19f), at the weekends because we live close to each other and our parents are friendly. She doesn’t have a car, and honestly, I regret agreeing to this arrangement, but I felt bad for her when she asked a few months ago and figured it’ll only be the weekends as she’s part time and goes to college during the week. Big mistake.

Here’s the thing: Megan doesn’t know how to shut up. From the second she gets in my car, she’s yapping about her cat, her college life, her weekend plans, her crazy dreams, or whatever random nonsense pops into her head. It’s nonstop. I’ve tried putting on music or even outright saying, “Hey, I like quiet in the mornings,” but she just giggles and keeps going.

Yesterday was the last straw. I picked her up as usual, and within five minutes, she was already going on about how she “might start a podcast” because people tell her she has “such interesting stories.” I couldn’t take it anymore. I told her, “Nobody cares about your podcast, Megan,” but she just laughed and said, “You’re so grumpy in the mornings!”

At this point, I’d had it. We were about halfway to work when she started talking about how she was asking her parents if she could foster a dog to keep her cat company. I snapped and said, “You know what, Megan? Get out.” She thought I was joking, but I wasn’t. I pulled over to a gas station and told her to get out of my car. She started freaking out, saying she didn’t have another way to get to work, but I told her that wasn’t my problem.

She got out (still whining). She showed up to workan hour late because she had to wait for an Uber which got her in trouble with management which is the least she deserves. Now people at work are acting like I’m the bad guy. A few coworkers said I was “out of line” and that I need to apologize, but why should I? She’s been annoying me for MONTHS. Her parents are now giving her lifts to and from work which is fine by me but my parents know her parents so somehow this whole incident got back to them and they’re giving out to me too and saying what I did was horrible. No one realizes how much she has been annoying me. AITA
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
B. Role-based Responsibility: The judgment is primarily justified by duties tied to a specific functional role (tenant/roommate/employee/co-parent) rather than emotion-based loyalty.
C. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
D. Fairness/Reciprocity: The judgment is primarily justified by proportionality, equity, or reciprocal treatment (fair share, equal standards, tit-for-tat).

context.scenario:
Throwaway because Im friends with a couple of my neighbors and know they have reddit, dont really want this linked to main.

I wfh and it’s usually pretty chill shifts so I tend to do chores in between tasks or meetings. Today I decided to do laundry at the beginning of my shift, since there was not really anything major planned for the day.

My building has 5 units that splits one washer and one dryer in the basement, so you have to time it properly to not run into or have to wait for another neighbor to finish up.

I threw my first load in, all good, moved it to the dryer and put my 2nd load in the washer. Then I get a message from my manager to come in for a meeting as client expectations for a project had changed and they wanted to update the team on the new guidelines. This meeting ended up going for a couple hours. There were intermittent short breaks where I couldve checked on laundry, both loads were definitely done by now, but I felt if I left my desk it’d leave a bad impression.

When I finally went downstairs, I found that my first load was taken out and put on top of the dryer, and my second load was moved from the washer, also dried, and now also sitting on the dryer. There were a different neighbor’s laundry currently going through their own cycle. There was also a sticky note about being sure to be on time with my laundry so others can use the machines.

First off, it was only a couple hours. Can other people just touch and move your laundry like that? What if some of the things I was washing were delicate and couldnt be run through the dryer, now their impatience has ruined them (luckily didnt have anything delicate). I figure it’s rude to touch others things when they couldve come back to check when I was done and then put their things in. Plus I was working so its not like I could be a perfect person with perfect timing, I had other obligations.

AITA for not immediately moving my laundry, or is my neighbor for touching and moving things that arent theirs? I feel like some basic understanding couldve been extended.
context.comment:
YTA - and you're stepping into being an entitled AH, so watch your step.

**Your words:**

***so you have to time it properly to not run into or have to wait for another neighbor to finish up.***

Then you say this happened:

*Then I get a message from my manager to come in for a meeting as client expectations for a project had changed and they wanted to update the team on the new guidelines.*

This is a you problem and no one else's.  This means that YOU are the one that needs to find a solution to meet the expectations that are set (see the "your words" section)

This is you gaslighting:

*First off, it was only a couple hours.*

To who?  You or the person waiting (see the "your words" section again) to use the washer/dryer.

As far as your questions at the end are concerned:

*Can other people just touch and move your laundry like that?*

Yes, yes they can because there are rules set in place to make sure that people like you don't get all entitled and think that you can just do what you want with communal property.

*What if some of the things I was washing were delicate and couldnt be run through the dryer, now their impatience has ruined them (luckily didnt have anything delicate).*

Once again, this is a YOU problem because if you didn't take advantage of the situation and follow the rules that have been set up(and accepted by you) none of this would happen.

And now for the entitlement:

*I figure it’s rude to touch others things when they couldve come back to check when I was done and then put their things in.* 

Of course you're going to figure that because you're being manipulative and deflecting on what the real problem is.  That problem being you think that others have to adjust to cater to your needs.

*Plus I was working so its not like I could be a perfect person with perfect timing, I had other obligations.*

This is 100% back on you again.  Who the fuck cares that YOU were working.  Is there something in the rules that say when you're working you can monopolize the washer and dyer?  No?  Didn't think so.

You are 100% to blame for all of this.  Stop thinking that you're not the problem and do better.

context.scenario:
AITA for not covering part of a hotel bill?

So me and four of my other friends went on a trip over the summer to LA. We decided to get a hotel room there and split the cost. My parents gave us some money to cover a large portion of the hotel room cost. This covered over half of cost of the hotel room and they did this because they wanted us to spend our money on having more of a good time rather than having to pay for a hotel room. One of my friends was initially paying the hotel cost on his credit card then we would pay him back. However, after I gave him the money from my parents, he said that my parents paying for a huge part of the hotel cost would definitely cover my part and I wouldn’t have to pay him back. I at no point said I shouldn’t be expected to pay my part, he was the one to say this to me without me prompting this idea. After we got back from the trip however, he said that he actually thought it was unfair that I was the only one not paying for part of the hotel. I told him he was the one to say that I didn’t have to pay, to which he said I should have at least offered. He continued to bring this up and he told me this made him question the way he saw me. This obviously made me feel really bad, because I do understand that my other friends had to pay. So in the end I did pay and When I paid they got back 15 dollars each. I still feel pretty awful about thinking I didn’t have to pay, but I just wanted to know AITA for initially believing that I shouldn’t have had to pay.


Update/edit: I wanted to add it was really only the friend who put their credit card down who made me feel as though I had to pay on top of what my parents gave- i just saw a lot of comments kinda hating on all my friends but they really didn’t pressure me to pay or say anything about it to me. They are really good friends to me and I just wanted to preface that.

Secondly, I left this part out of the story cause it seemed like a minor detail that didn’t matter but I also saw some comments about how the money was given to me (like if it was given to the group or what not). My parents had pulled me aside and gave me the money. My parents don’t like to flaunt money or anything so my dad did it more so discreetly but said it was for the hotel. 

Thank you guys for your opinions and advice I really appreciate it.
context.comment:
Sorry, OP YTA - I don't know why the people are saying that you're NTA when your parents said, and I'm copying what you wrote here:

*they did this because they wanted* ***us*** *to spend our money on having more of a good time rather than having to pay for a hotel room.*

**"US"**  as in all of you.  There is not grey area here.  It is crystal clear what the money your parents gave you was for.  Not just you.  That's why your friend is looking at you in a different way.  Your parents gave the money to the group and here you are thinking that is was just for you leaving the rest of your friends to pay their own way.  This goes against the reason your parents gave your GROUP the money.  You got greedy and got called out.

context.scenario:
i 23f just sat in an argument between my mother (some age F) and my sister 28F.

she has always been very emotionally manipulative and i believe my short temper developed because i could not stand her.

today the argument was because my sister was planning her wedding and then suddenly my mom was like ok ill be inviting these family friends. my sister knows of them but we have not seen these folks since like we were in middle school. my sister wants to prioritize her friends in college and longtime friends over these family friends.

my mom is upset my sister essentially said no to her inviting extra people. my mom is helping out with costs and said she’s not giving any money if she can’t invite people and that my sister saying this already upsets her so now she will not do any of it.

my sisters point is that this is her wedding and she and her fiance can invite anyone they want to, and if there’s extra space they you can invite family friends. but she doubts there will be extra space based on budget. my mom has traditional culture tendencies that i cave to at times and it’s annoying as hell.

i was just fed up with her thinking she runs the show. they’re still arguing and all i want to do is tell my mom to stop talking and ask her if it’s because she had an uneventful wedding and now needs to live her life through my sisters wedding. WIBTA if i said that? or am i just saying what needs to be said
context.comment: NTA - your mom is giving your sister the money so your sister can have HER own wedding.  Your mom isn't giving you sister the money so your mom can throw a party for her friends.  I know it sucks but tell you sister to stand her ground on telling mom "no".  This will probably be the reason your mother pulls the funding which is shy your mom is a massive, insecure AH.  Your sister will need to take a step back and start from scratch with the planning on the new budget BUT it means that your mom has literally zero (0) say in ANYTHING your sister plans.  Best of luck, OP.

context.scenario:
This whole things feels absurd because of how inane it is. I work at a building with a parking garage and assigned spots. I almost always park in my spot on the top floor, but it is uncovered. Currently, more than half of the people who park in this garage are gone for the season, so they will very seldom use their spots and never all at once. Today, we had a torrential downpour while I was coming into work, so I chose to park in one of the hundreds of empty covered spots. I knew no one would have an issue with it, and if someone did come in that normally parked in that spot, they could park to the right or left of me and that would be that. It really shouldn't be a life or death issue here. 

I got a phone call from my boss a few hours into the workday that the parking garage security guard is planning to have my car towed if I don't move it right this second. I go down to the garage and I'm met with a very indignant old man screaming at me for "thinking the rules don't apply to me." I will admit, that rule doesn't seem like one that needs to be followed at all costs, so yes, I don't believe that "rule" should be enforced this way. Maybe I'm wrong.

Now I've become the subject of a lengthy email chain sent to everyone in the building about parking in designated spots no matter what the weather or circumstances. I cannot conceive a reason why this should have everyone so up in arms, but that's where I'm at right now. 

To reiterate, I normally park in my assigned spot and only don't when I know beyond a doubt that it won't be a problem and I have a genuine reason not to, such as the weather. How stupid is it to walk into work soaking wet when there are hundreds of empty, covered spots? 

Furthermore, I drive a brand new car. I don't say that because it entitles me to park where I want, but maybe some can understand why I don't want to park where I might get hailed on when that can be avoided.
context.comment: YTA - What you failed to mention is how disrespectful it is to make that decision just for your convenience.  See,  you just parked thinking nothing of it.  Very entitled.  Assigned means that you do NOT have the right to make any decisions regarding that spot.  As you found out, it actually is monitored.  You just showed that you do not follow the rules and think that you can just "do" because you want to.  Everything in this post makes you look very entitled.  Follow the rules at work or get disciplined.  That's it.  Everything with you having a new car and this and that is just you cementing your entitlement.

context.scenario:
My partner and I recently bought our first home. During the summer, we decided to clean up the backyard, it was a bit overgrown and messy, so we wanted to get it looking nice and start a garden.

There’s a fence between our property and our next door neighbor’s. During the cleanup, we cut down a Manitoba Maple that had started growing right along the fence line on our side. It wasn’t a planned tree, more like something that had seeded itself and started growing wild, it was about 6 feet high and the diameter of the trunk was about 3 inches, and we figured we’d replace it with flowers and some landscaping.

It turns out the tree wasn’t actually on our property. Apparently, when the neighbor put up the fence, he didn’t build it on the actual property line, it was built a few feet into his own yard. So even though the tree looked like it was on our side of the fence, it was technically on his land. We had no idea.

When he noticed the tree was gone, he came outside visibly angry and started yelling at us. We apologized right away and explained that we genuinely believed it was on our property, and shared our plan to replace it with flowers and landscaping. Unfortunately, he wasn’t satisfied. He’s actually the tenant, and he reported the situation to his landlord, who then sent us a letter stating they intend to pursue legal action.

On top of that, we’re now frustrated because, due to how the fence is placed, we have to maintain this strip of land on “our” side of the fence; mowing, weeding, dealing with plants, even though it’s technically still his property. It doesn’t feel fair that we’re responsible for upkeep on a piece of land that we don’t actually own and can’t make decisions about.

AITA for accidentally cutting down my neighbor’s tree, but offering to replace it with other landscaping?

EDIT: I really appreciate everyone's input here and I wanted to add some new information here to add based on some comments.

* We do not live in the states and where we live it is not mandatory to get a survey done when buying a home.
* Based on Google Maps street view, the fence has been there since at least 2019.
* I know in the post I said we'd replace the tree with flowers and other landscaping, but we also did mention a new tree (I should have mentioned that as well)
* I 100% agree that we weren't smart and made a stupid assumption about the property line based on where the fence was placed.

EDIT#2:

* Something else came to mind while reading the comments, before this whole incident, we had actually asked the tenant for his landlord’s contact info because we wanted to improve the shared space in the front yard. He refused to give it to us and told us not to worry, saying the landlord would be fine with us doing whatever we wanted.
context.comment: YTA - Welcome to being a home owner.  How can you not know where your property line is?  This is like day one stuff.  It is up to you to find that out.  You can't just go blindly by what you see and start chopping trees down.  Especially that close to the property line.  You see others doing it to their properties because THEY PUT IN THE WORK TO KNOW WHERE THE PROPERTY LINE IS.  You just went out back with a chainsaw.  Negligence is no excuse and you need to contact your lawyer because the landlord does have cause.

context.scenario:
So much for 'doing the right thing'.

Yesterday, my kids' school held a picnic at a neighborhood park to welcome kids back to the new school year. While parking in a tight spot, I accidentally backed into the corner of a Honda minivan. No one was in the minivan at the time, but I had seen the father in a previous loop through the parking area. Feeling bad, I decided to 'do the right thing' and find him at the playground to let him know. We walked over to the car, I showed him the bump, gave him my cel number and told him to forward me the repair bill.

Later in the day, I received a text from him that said 'Because our 3 car seats were in the accident, we have to replace them. The cost will be $839.97".

I was f-ing flabbergasted. The minivan was barely dented in the corner and no one was even in the vehicle at the time...and yet this guy expects me to pay the cost for replacing his three kids' car seats, each of which is apparently the cadillac of all car seats at $280 a pop??? WTF???

  
I wrote him back that I was not comfortable with that. I told him I had come to him honorably to right a wrong, and that I hoped his expectations would match the same spirit. He responded saying that him and his wife are uncomfortable driving their children in unsafe car seats and that they need to do what is safest for their children.

I think the guy is acting like a woke, entitled pr\*ck. Who is the ahole here?
context.comment: NTA - I don't think the people responding with "YTA" are aware of something.  She bumped, not crashed, into the minivan causing very minimal damage to the corner of the bumper.  She did do the right thing and admitted fault to the owner.  Somehow the owner feels that the slight fender bender was enough to tag on a set of car seats that they feel are needed to keep their children safe.  OP is in no way shape or form responsible for the car seats because there was no damage to the car seats that were a result of the fender bender.  Therefore, OP has every right to say "NO" to them trying to scam OP.  That's what this is, a scam.  You are right that OP did not need to put the "woke" comment in.  That would be them fishing for a label and is just doesn't work.  At the end of the day, OP is responsible for the damages done to the bumper(which they admitted fault and will pay to repair) but not the car seats.  That part is the person trying to scam OP.

context.scenario:
( tl;dr -- negotiating my share of repair cost, considering housemates have used it a year longer than me, and make more regular use of it. They want me to pay more than the 20% I offered)

So our dishwasher needs to be repaired. It's likely a wear and tear issue.

For a year and a half, I've lived with two housemates, who've both lived here a year longer than me. Throughout the time we've lived together, I haven't regularly used the dishwasher, instead, I mostly handwash.  
I'd estimate that with the average of them running the dishwasher twice a week, I'll use it maybe twice a month. Except for the odd bottle or two that I throw in here and there.

They've asked that we split the cost three ways, which I've said doesn't seem equitable. They're okay with me paying a lesser percentage, however, we're negotiating exactly how much.

I don't plan on paying higher than 20% of the cost, but they're asking for more. Frankly, I use the appliance so infrequently that this hasn't made much difference to me  
  
AITA?

More context:  
\-The lease says that Landlord isn't responsible  
\-The dishwasher came with the apartment, I don't know how old  
\-Repair cost will be a minimum of $200, most likely more
context.comment: YTA - whether you're putting a full load in or just one cup with the rest, you're using it.  Every time you put that cup in there, that counts as as much usage as them.  The dishwasher doesn't care how many of your dishes are in there.  It's being used.  20-25% is fair.

context.scenario:
My parents paid for my brother and mine degrees. But my brother went to med school and I just did an undergrad. I’m not sure of the exact number but they gave at least $200k On TOP of his undergrad and my undergrad was $64k. They promised they would help me with my first house and they did. They gave me $40k to match the 40k I had already saved for a down payment for my first house and they gave us $10k for our wedding. 

But that’s the extent of the help. My brother now finished residency and is making 300-400k and my husband is having to retire from being a plumber at 44 because of a bad knee. He cannot get up after kneeling anymore. My oldest is 13 and we don’t have anything saved for college because my husband and I didn’t make much in the last couple years. He would need time off to recover from knee surgery and I only make $23/h. Both my SIL and brother have professional jobs they can do well into their 60s and my husband is having to reskill into something that isnt physical but he doesn’t have any high ed. Right now he is looking at being a housing inspector which will take about a year to get the training and hours. I’m solely support our family and we cannot keep up with bills. My parents sold their house during the pandemic and now have an apartment in Atlanta but they travel around. I’ve asked them for help but they said retirement use up most of their savings and they probably will only have the apartment to pass onto us. I asked for an advance of any inheritance but they refused justifying it by saying they don’t know how much will be left. I told them about our situation and they said they cannot help us. My parents are 66 and 67 they could still work and they are spending a lot of money traveling when we are struggling. I argued my brother got a lot more help than we did and the down payment doesn’t in any way cover the extreme about of money they spent toward my brother. They argued they encouraged me to do a masters and they would have paid for it but I didn’t and it’s too late to expect more. I’m quite pissed because we would be in a different place if they had really made things equal between my brother and I but AITA for expecting my parents to help me since they had my brother more in the past
context.comment:
YTA - It's their money and their life.  If they gave you any money, what money would they have to support themselves with for just the basic essentials.  Remember, they don't work.  They don't owe you a damn thing.  That was then, this is now.

This is your life now.  You don't get to go back 10 years and start adding up the receipts.  Life sucks but it's your life.  You are now living through the consequences of your actions by not getting a better education/career as well as your husband choosing the path of manual labor.  Does that make you bad people, of course not.  But it also doesn't mean that anybody, and I mean anybody, has to pick up your slack.  If you need to, find a better paying job.  Your husband can doordash/uber to make some extra cash if need be.  Stop hounding your parents.  It's their money and their life and again, they don't owe you anything.  Stop thinking they do and start being proactive in figuring this out on your own.

context.scenario: So my friend (19m) was going to host a BBQ tomorrow to celebrate him finishing his military service and he told my friend group today that his friends from his school (all 19-20m) would be there, and upon hearing that we were a bit skeptical, because we don't like them, for several reasons. So we were 5 people sitting in Discord, he wasn't present, and discussing how we should tell him that we might not show up if they are there. So then I sent him a text saying that I can be with them and that I will still be nice and have a fun time, but that I do not like his other friends and also that most of my friends don't either and might not even show up. It was meant as a heads up, so that he wouldn't simply wonder what he did wrong, to show that he didn't do anything bad, just that we don't like his other friends. I also said that it serioulsy might be a good idea to split it into two separate celebrations with the two different friend groups, because we don't like them and they don't like us. He then jumped into discord where me and two of the friends sat (plus another one that lived too far away to come regardless) and told us that if wedon't want to be there if then we couldn't come either, which is opposite to what I said (explicitly telling him that I would still be there and content), but since I didn't want to further outrage him I didn't tell him that he had misinterpreted me either.
context.comment:
YTA - *I didn't tell him that he had misinterpreted me either.*

See, where you went wrong is sending the text on behalf of the others that you were on discord with.  By doing that, you lumped yourself into that circle and not your own person.

All you had to do was so "Sorry, Bro.  won't be able to make it."  If he asks why, tell him the truth.  What you don't get to do is speak for the others.  That is up to them to answer the guy throwing the party.  Not you.

context.scenario:
I have a townhouse at the beach that we only use on weekends. It is next to a house that's used as a short-term rental. My house and the short-term rental share a small front yard (e.g., the property line runs down the middle of the yard), with each house's driveway on its respective side of the yard. We frequently see people from the short-term rental walking through the yard and across our driveway which, while annoying, is not really an issue.

Last night, I kept getting alerts on my security camera that there was motion in my driveway. I logged into the camera, and a large group of people (I counted at least 13), most of whom were kids, were hanging out in the driveway of the short-term rental and spilling over onto my side of the yard and my driveway. Kids were doing back flips in our yard, running around our driveway, etc. It was basically chaos. I let it go on for a few minutes as I was hoping that they would stop without intervention, but after two kids face-planted in my driveway I obviously had to shut it down. I spoke to them through the camera and told them they weren't allowed to be in my driveway, and they quickly left. 

I was concerned that if these people are staying there for the next few days, it would continue to be an issue so I then texted a few screenshots of kids on the ground in my driveway to the owner of the short-term rental and said, "This is a problem." I was expecting him to say, "I'll ask them to stay off your property," or something to that effect. Instead, he defended them and said it was "kids being kids" and a "family enjoying the outdoors." He also tried to say that if I don't have a problem with people walking across my driveway, I shouldn't have a problem with what happened last night. I seriously felt like I was taking crazy pills. After some back-and-forth, I ended the exchange with, "I do not want your tenants on my property. End of story." 

I don't think I'm in the wrong, but the fact that he thinks I am being unreasonable for having a problem with this is making me want to get some outside perspective. Also, there is a large park literally right across the street from the house where they could go play.

(To answer an anticipated question, yes, I am putting in a fence to divide the yard. I have someone coming to do an estimate tomorrow.)
context.comment: NTA - find out where it's listed and leave a scathing review of the owner.  Doesn't matter how nice of a place it is or where it's at.  No one wants to deal with an AH owner.  If the owner flips his lid, remind him of property lines and insurance claims and the fact you have a lawyer.

context.scenario:
So, about six months ago, my partner and I saw someone moving in downstairs as a new neighbor. We'll call the person Tammy. We hung out with them and found out that they were actually the new roommate of someone already staying here.

We find out, a month later, that the person living here didn't want Tammy as her roo.mate anymore. That person also called the cops on Tammy to get her removed. The cops came, said she needed time to get her things out and, well my partner asked if I wanted Tammy to stay with us for a little bit until she gets her things together. Tammy said that she could pay us rent for a room. We find out soon that Tammy has a lot of shit that she moved into the other apartment that she wanted to move into ours. So, then it becomes a whole thing of taking her things to storage. We had to rearrange our apartment three times just to have space for everything and Tammy is still bringing things in. I personally think they have hoarder tendencies. They have depression and ADHD but they are not getting help or medication. 

Fast forward six months and they have barely taken any excess stuff to storage, they eat our food, they complain about us cleaning out food from the fridge from months ago, and now they're spreading their things into the upstairs neighbor's apartment, taking over their deck. I confronted them today, after they screamed at me about not replacing several frozen food items, that were clogging the freezer and that she didn't touch since she moved in, about evicting her. Tammy made excuses as to why she didn't communicate why she didn't say anything back when I originally cleaned the fridge, and showed her, including her ADHD, her depression, her forgetfulness, and not wanting to "kill my joy" by saying something. 

Tammy doesn't have a job. She paid us in savings. Her family cut her off. Her friends won't help her. She's arguing with my partner, who wants to kick her out too. I feel so disrespected and taken advantage of. So I told Tammt about her being evicted and she immediately brought up me being manipulative by doing that, brought up all the faults of my partner and how I should evict her, how I'm not being fair, and how she is already doing all she can and I can't ask more from her. I'm very non-confrontational and I'm unsure if I'm doing the right thing. I'm also terrified of retaliation, so I'm second-guessi g things. Tammy brought up how she has issues with housing and has nobody and how cruel I'm being.

But I bought her Christmas presents when I had none. I pay for her storage. I pay for food, for her phone line because her parents cut her off, for all the bills and our debts. I handle everything. And she says she cleans cat litter every day. She hasn't gotten state or federal assistance. And she won't try for a job unless it pays 25-30 dollars an hours. She says she has a degree and she won't take less.

Am I the asshole for giving her 30 days to get her shit together before I move forward with an eviction notice?
context.comment: YTA - to yourselves.  The neighbors had to call the cops to get her to leave and this is where you and your partner think to yourselves "Let's offer this stranger a place to stay".  The warning signs were there and you ignored them and brought all of this on yourselves.  You can give her 30 days notice but if she stays and you have to evict her, she's there for another 6 months at least because of the court systems.  Best of luck, OP.

context.scenario: I don't know how to do this exactly, first time here. My daughter (mid/early 20s) lives at home and hasn't paid rent for years because I thought she was struggling financially. She now has a good job, just bought a car that's like 3 years old so I figure she can afford $150 a month in rent. She seems angry that I'm now asking her to pay rent. I'm doing good financially (46/f) and my boyfriend is going to move in and he told me he is also going to contribute to finances without me asking him to. Is it common for adult Gen z to not pay rent while living at home?
context.comment: NTA - doesn't matter what generation it is.  Doesn't matter that it's the parents house.  You become an adult, you pay rent.  It is very arrogant to think that she doesn't have to pay rent just because she's living at home.  Especially with a job that has afforded her a new car.  $150 also seems a little low.  I'd start there and increase it in time(all in writing).  Maybe she will take the hint and move on with her life and get a place of her own(or with friends).  It's time she starts taking care of herself.  Like us Gen X and Boomers do lol.(jk - just wanted to have fun with "generations")

scenario: They texted us two hours before the tree fell saying they noticed that it was splitting and were going to have it removed. Two hours later, the tree split and fell on our fence and into our yard, and suddenly, they're not going to pay for the removal on our side. Am I wrong for expecting them to repair the fence that we paid to have installed and remove the tree? A storm didn't cause this; it was just an old tree that split down the middle.
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
B. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
C. Safety/Risk Management: The judgment is primarily justified by risk-taking, danger creation, or failure to follow safety precautions, even if no harm occurred.
D. Care/Harm Prevention: The judgment is primarily justified by concern about direct physical or emotional suffering of a person (victim-centered empathy, trauma, distress).
